name: auto-classified-permission
description: Claude Code running in auto mode receives a prompt that triggers a benign tool call (Bash 'echo hello' or Read on a project file) which the in-product auto-classifier approves without firing a permission prompt. The daemon must see ready→working→ready with no transition to waiting at any point. Locks down the safe-path branch of the March 2026 Auto Mode feature; the risky-path branch is owned by tool-gate-permission-prompt.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable
driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable
driver_capability: ready

## What the scenario requires

The `permission_hooks` primitive here is specifically a *classifier*: the agent inspects each tool call and selectively prompts the user only for the risky ones, while safe calls run without ever entering `waiting`. The user-observable distinguisher is two divergent paths from the SAME session: safe → `ready → working → ready` (no `waiting`), risky → `working → waiting → working → ready` after approval. The scenario is the safe-path branch; the risky-path branch is owned by `tool-gate-permission-prompt`. Both branches must be driven by a content-aware classifier, not a blanket policy (that is the explicit "Not to be confused with" carve-out vs `auto-executed-tool-call`, which auto-approves everything, and `tool-gate-permission-prompt`, which gates everything).

## Why agent_supports is partial, not no or yes

Aider HAS a permission gate, but it is uniform, not classifier-based. All confirmations funnel through a single `IO.confirm_ask` (aider/io.py:807). Its entire decision logic is the run flag: `if self.yes is True: res = "y"` / `elif self.yes is False: res = "n"` / else interactively prompt (io.py:866-868). `--yes-always` (aider/args.py:760, help: "Always say yes to every confirmation") sets `self.yes = True` blanket. There is no branch that inspects the tool/command content to decide safe-vs-risky — a `strings`-equivalent grep of the installed 0.86.2 package for `classif|risky|dangerous|safe.?command|allowlist|denylist` matched only an unrelated docs file (ollama.md), and the only auto-* flags are `--yes-always` and `--auto-accept-architect` (neither content-aware).

So the verdict is not `no` (aider genuinely has a permission-confirmation mechanism and can run the safe topology) and not `yes` (the mechanism is blanket, not the selective classifier the scenario's primitive demands). `partial` with the gap named is the honest call, ratifying the prior matrix note "/yes-always is blanket, not classifier-based."

## Why daemon_capability is incapable

The defining behavior of this scenario — a content-aware classifier that diverts a *risky* call into `working → waiting` within the same session while running safe calls straight through — does not exist in aider, so there is no classifier-shaped behavior for any parser to observe. Beyond the missing feature, the transport leaves no trace of permission decisions at all: `Source` is `FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser}` (agent.go:45-51); the markdown parser's `toolCall()` emits the tool open and close in a single `tool_result` event "so the state classifier sees a completed tool call without a permission window" (parser.go:231-242). No parser path emits a held-open tool_use / permission / `waiting` event — the only states aider can produce are `working` (turn open) and `ready` (turn_done via IdleFlush or error). `capabilities.json` carries `"permission_hooks": false`. This is `incapable` (no trace + agent has nothing classifier-shaped to produce), not `bug`: there is no mis-handled trace in `.aider.chat.history.md` to point at, because aider's interactive idle confirmation prompt is never written to the history file in the first place.

Note the safe-path topology alone (`ready → working → ready`, no `waiting`) WOULD be observable — but that is indistinguishable from the `auto-executed-tool-call` scenario. The thing that DEFINES auto-classified-permission — the classifier's risky-path divergence within the same session — is what cannot be observed.

## Why driver_capability is ready

The driver is not the blocker. `scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh` implements send / slash / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep, which is the full set of step types a safe-path recipe would need (send a prompt that triggers a benign Edit/Bash, wait_turn for the synthesized idle-flush turn_done). No gapped primitive (restart, sigkill, keys, fork, session…) is on the recipe path. `driver_capability: ready` correctly isolates the blocker to architecture (the missing feature + no transport trace), not tooling — so this routes `frozen`, not `driver-gap`.

## What would change this

If aider ever shipped a content-aware command-safety classifier (selectively prompting only for risky shell commands while auto-running safe ones), this would become `agent_supports: yes` and `daemon_capability` would need a parser change: the markdown history would have to surface a confirmation prompt the parser could map to a held-open tool_use, since today aider's interactive idle confirmation prompt never lands in `.aider.chat.history.md` at all.
caveats: feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant: even the blanket --yes-always confirmation prompt is not written to .aider.chat.history.md, so irrlicht has no transcript signal for any permission decision, classifier-based or not.

The safe-path topology (ready→working→ready) IS observable, but it is identical to auto-executed-tool-call; the classifier's risky-path divergence is what is unobservable, and it does not exist in aider.

sources.note: toolCall() (lines 231-242) emits tool open+close in one tool_result "without a permission window"; no parser path emits a waiting/permission event — anchors daemon_capability: incapable
Source is FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser}; adapter assumes aider runs under --yes-always; no held-open tool_use trace in the watched file
permission_hooks: false — the Primitive-exercised key for this scenario
confirm_ask (line 807): only decision is self.yes True/False/interactive (lines 866-868) — uniform, no content classifier
--yes-always (line 760) "Always say yes to every confirmation" — blanket; only auto-* flags are --yes-always and --auto-accept-architect, neither content-aware
Aider 0.86.2 options reference: --yes-always is the sole blanket-approve flag; no safe-vs-risky classifier documented

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

Claude Code ships an in-product 'auto mode' (footer 'auto mode on', driver waits for this string before sending input — see drive-claudecode-interactive.sh). In auto mode the runtime's classifier auto-approves safe tool calls (Read, Grep, contained Edit, contained Bash within allow-listed paths) and still gates risky ones (writes to .git/.claude/.vscode/.idea/.husky, network-egress shells, broad bash patterns) through the normal permission prompt. So the agent_supports answer is unambiguous yes — the feature exists and ships in current TUI builds.

irrlicht observes the two paths via independent transports and they collapse onto exactly the two state arcs the spec demands.

**Safe (auto-approved) tool call — `ready → working → ready`, never `waiting`.** A safe tool_use appears in the JSONL transcript, the tailer marks `HasOpenToolCall=true`, ClassifyState returns `working` (rule 4). When the tool_result arrives in the same transcript, `HasOpenToolCall` goes back to false and the eventual `stop_reason=end_turn` (or `system/turn_duration` event mapped to `turn_done`) lets `IsAgentDone()` fire → `working → ready` (rule 2b). Crucially Claude Code does NOT fire a PermissionRequest hook for an auto-approved call (hooks.go HookPermissionRequest only dispatches when the runtime actually shows the prompt), so `PermissionPending` never gets set and rule 0 never trips. The waiting state cannot appear.

**Risky tool call — `working → waiting`, then resume.** When the classifier decides the call needs user approval, Claude Code fires the PermissionRequest hook ahead of (or alongside) the tool_use JSONL event. The hook receiver in `hooks.go` dispatches into `SessionDetector.HandlePermissionHook`, which sets `state.Metrics.PermissionPending = true` (session_detector_activity.go:346). On the next classifier pass rule 0 transitions to `waiting` (state_classifier.go:32). When the user approves, Claude Code fires PostToolUse (or PostToolUseFailure on deny — observably identical to irrlicht); the receiver clears the flag, classifier returns to `working`, and the eventual turn_done returns to `ready`. This is exactly the contract the spec asserts.

## What irrlicht observes about the classifier itself

Nothing — and intentionally. irrlicht does not inspect Claude Code's classifier decision; it only sees the downstream observable (hook fired vs not). That's the correct boundary: the classifier's verdict is upstream policy. The spec also only asserts the downstream state arcs, so the assessment is irrlicht_observes=yes with no transport gap.

## Caveats

The hook installer (hookinstaller.go) and statusline installer must be in place for the hook path to wire up — that's part of the daemon's first-run setup and not scenario-specific. The recipe must drive Claude Code in auto mode (the driver script already handles that). The recipe should mix at least one safe + one risky call to exercise both branches in the same session.
caveats: Requires Claude Code in 'auto mode' (driver script sends `<shift>+<tab>` and waits for `auto mode on` in the footer); a recipe that forgets to enable auto mode would exercise the normal permission-prompt path instead and the safe-call assertion (never enters waiting) would no longer be meaningful.

The risky call must target a path or command the classifier actually gates. Future tightening or loosening of Claude Code's allow-list could turn a previously-risky shell into an auto-approved one and silently flip the second arc to never-waiting. The recipe should pick a stable gate trigger (e.g. write under `.git/`).

The Stage-1 spec name maps to 'Auto-classified permission approval' (March 2026 Claude Code Auto Mode). Older Claude Code releases without auto mode would map to a different cell (Tool gate via permission prompt) — agents below 2.1.112 may not surface the auto-classified path at all.

sources.note: PermissionRequest/PostToolUse hook handler: PermissionRequest dispatches when Claude Code actually shows a prompt (i.e. classifier did NOT auto-approve); auto-approved calls bypass this path entirely.

Rule 0 (`PermissionPending → waiting`) is the only path that can flip a session to waiting from the hook. Without a PermissionRequest the rule never fires and the session stays on the rule-4 → rule-2b working→ready arc.

Line 346: `state.Metrics.PermissionPending = true` is set only via the hook receiver — there is no parser-side path to this flag, which is why auto-approved JSONL tool_use events cannot accidentally flip it.
Driver supports `auto mode on` footer detection — the recipe primitive needed to put Claude Code into the classifier-driven mode for this scenario.

preconditions: claude CLI on PATH and authenticated (subscription or API key) — must be a release that ships Auto Mode (>= 2.1.112)
irrlichd is recording (--attach against a daemon with --record, or isolated mode)
Hook installer and statusline installer in place (daemon first-run setup) so the PermissionRequest hook path is wired
setup: Driver generates a fresh lowercased session UUID and a fresh cwd under <staging>/cwd/
Driver enables auto mode (Shift+Tab until 'auto mode on' appears in the footer) before the first send

The tool call MUST be one the classifier auto-approves — a benign Bash 'echo' or a Read of a project-local file. Do NOT target .git/.claude/.vscode/.idea/.husky or network-egress shells (those flip to the risky-path scenario)

script.text: Run bash 'echo hello' using the Bash tool, then reply 'done'.

verify: events.jsonl shows the UUID session entering ready, then exactly one ready→working transition
events.jsonl contains at least one tool_use event for the Bash tool inside the working span (proves the tool call actually fired)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the UUID at any point (classifier auto-approved → PermissionRequest hook never fired → PermissionPending never set)
events.jsonl shows exactly one working→ready transition at turn end
transcript.jsonl contains 1 user line and at least 2 assistant lines (tool_use + final reply)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready
applicable: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready
applicable: n/a

## Reasoning

`auto-classified-permission` (scenario-meanings.md): the defining behavior is an **internal classifier that auto-approves SAFE tool calls but GATES risky ones**, so within a single turn the user observes `ready → working → ready` for the safe calls (never `waiting`) AND a `working → waiting` for the risky one, then `waiting → working → ready` after explicit user approval. The **Primitive exercised is `permission_hooks`** — the selective safe-vs-risky discrimination is the whole point. It is explicitly *Not to be confused with* `auto-executed-tool-call` (ALL calls auto-approve, never any waiting) or `tool-gate-permission-prompt` (EVERY call requires approval).

**agent_supports: partial.** Codex CLI 0.130.0 has an `approval_policy` (parser_test.go references `"approval_policy": "never"`) and a sandbox: under its default policy benign shell/exec commands auto-execute with no prompt, while commands that escalate out of the sandbox surface a separate approval. So codex DOES auto-run some commands and gate others — but this is a coarse sandbox/escalation boundary, not the canonical scenario's per-call "safe vs risky" classifier that selectively prompts WITHIN one turn. `capabilities.json` records `permission_hooks: false`: codex ships no auto-permission classifier hook of the kind this scenario exercises. Hence `partial`, not `yes`.

**daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: ready.** This is the load-bearing verdict. The codex parser (parser.go) has exactly two `waiting`-producing paths, neither of which is a safe-vs-risky classifier signal: (1) a `<proposed_plan>` assistant block mapped to `ExitPlanMode` (that is `user-blocking-plan-mode-approval`), and (2) a `function_call` left without a `function_call_output` at `task_complete` → `HasOpenToolCall=true` → generic open-tool-call waiting (that is the `tool-gate-permission-prompt` / open-call shape — see `TestParser_ProposedPlan_EndToEndWaitingState`). There is NO transcript signal, and no parser branch, that lets the daemon distinguish "the classifier auto-approved this safe call but gated that risky one" — `permission_hooks: false`. So irrlicht cannot observe the scenario's defining selective-gating signal for codex.

The only codex-observable half of this scenario — a benign command auto-executing with no `waiting` (`ready → working → ready`) — is **already fully captured by the committed `codex × auto-executed-tool-call` cell** (and its sibling `full-lifecycle-toolcall`, coverage_id auto-executed-tool-call, recorded 6/6). A codex `auto-classified-permission` recording would be observationally identical to that cell and would assert nothing new, because the distinguishing primitive (selective `working → waiting` for a risky call within the same turn) is exactly what the daemon cannot see for codex. Recording it would prove the wrong thing (the auto-executed happy path, already covered) under the wrong label.

## Conclusion

The cell is frozen at `applicable: n/a`: codex has an approval mechanism (so not `no` on the agent), but irrlicht observes no signal that distinguishes this scenario from the already-covered `auto-executed-tool-call`. No recipe, no recording. The auto-classified safe/risky discrimination is a `permission_hooks` (claudecode Auto Mode) concept with no codex analogue the daemon can surface. This would unblock only if codex emitted a structured per-call classification event AND the codex parser learned to translate it into a selective `waiting` transition.
caveats: The auto-approved happy-path half of this scenario IS observable for codex, but it is already locked down by the committed `codex × auto-executed-tool-call` cell — a separate `auto-classified-permission` codex recording would duplicate it without exercising the distinguishing selective-gate signal.

If a future codex version surfaces a structured safe-vs-risky classification event (and the codex parser maps the risky branch to a selective `working → waiting`), re-assess — the verdict could lift to partial/partial and become applicable.

sources.note: auto-classified-permission: classifier selectively gates risky calls (working→waiting) while safe calls stay working; primitive = permission_hooks; explicitly distinct from auto-executed-tool-call
permission_hooks: false — codex ships no auto-permission classifier hook of the kind this scenario exercises
only waiting-producing paths are <proposed_plan>→ExitPlanMode and open function_call at task_complete; no safe-vs-risky classifier branch
approval_policy field present in fixtures; TestParser_ProposedPlan_EndToEndWaitingState shows the only generic open-tool-call→waiting path
the observable (no-waiting) half of this scenario is already captured by the codex auto-executed-tool-call cell — a codex auto-classified-permission recording would be a redundant near-duplicate

recipe.notes: applicable: n/a (assessment 2026-05-23: agent_supports=partial, irrlicht_observes=no). This scenario's defining signal is a per-call safe-vs-risky classifier that selectively prompts (working→waiting) for risky calls only — primitive permission_hooks, which codex has set false. Codex has a coarse sandbox/approval_policy that auto-runs benign commands and escalates others, but the codex parser exposes no safe-vs-risky classification: its only waiting paths are <proposed_plan>→ExitPlanMode (plan-mode-approval) and a generic open function_call at task_complete (open-tool-call/gate). The daemon therefore cannot observe the selective-gating signal that distinguishes this cell from auto-executed-tool-call. The only codex-observable half (a benign command auto-running with no waiting, ready→working→ready) is already fully captured by the committed codex × auto-executed-tool-call cell; recording it here would be a redundant near-duplicate that proves the wrong thing. No recipe, no recording. See replaydata/agents/codex/scenarios/auto-classified-permission/assessment.json.

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: yes (opencode has a config-driven per-call permission classifier: allow / ask / deny, with bash wildcard patterns)
daemon_capability: full (safe-path arc ready→working→ready, no waiting — proven observable on the ProcessOwnedStore transport)
driver_capability: ready (the SAFE branch this cell asserts needs NO interactive approval — it is config-auto-classified, so the prior gap:permission-approval no longer applies)

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes (opencode has a config-driven per-call permission classifier: allow / ask / deny, with bash wildcard patterns)
daemon_capability: full (safe-path arc ready→working→ready, no waiting — proven observable on the ProcessOwnedStore transport)
driver_capability: ready (the SAFE branch this cell asserts needs NO interactive approval — it is config-auto-classified, so the prior gap:permission-approval no longer applies)

## What this cell asserts

auto-classified-permission is the SAFE-PATH branch of permission classification: a benign tool call is AUTO-APPROVED without firing a permission prompt, and the session traces `ready → working → ready`, never entering `waiting`. The risky-path branch (the prompt actually firing → `waiting`) is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell. The claudecode sibling's expected.jsonl pins exactly this: a `no_waiting_window` invariant across the whole turn with `no state_transition to waiting`.

## Re-derivation (this reassessment): the driver gap is NOT relevant to the safe branch

The prior verdict (partial / full / gap:permission-approval) treated opencode's classifier as TUI-only and concluded the safe path was unrecordable headlessly. Re-deriving against opencode v1.14.50 + settings_file_support overturns the driver-gap half for THIS cell:

1. opencode's permission system IS config-driven (settings_file_support:true). The `permission` block in opencode.json keys tools (`bash`, `edit`, `read`, `webfetch`, …) to `"allow"` / `"ask"` / `"deny"`, and `bash` supports per-command wildcard patterns (`"git *": "allow"`, `"git commit *": "deny"`). This is a real AUTO-classifier: a per-call safe-vs-risky decision driven by static config, NOT only the TUI overlay. (opencode.ai/docs/permissions/.)
2. For the SAFE branch a benign tool set to `"allow"` (or matching an allow-wildcard) AUTO-EXECUTES with NO prompt → `ready → working → ready`, never `waiting`. v1.14.50 also ships `opencode run --dangerously-skip-permissions` ("auto-approve permissions that are not explicitly denied"), confirmed in `opencode run --help` on the installed binary — a headless YOLO auto-classify-approve that needs no interactive answer at all.
3. Therefore the safe path is fully recordable on the EXISTING headless driver: `drive-opencode-interactive.sh` already implements `send` + `wait_turn` over `opencode run`, and the auto-classified-approve needs only a settings file carrying an `allow` permission policy (or the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag). NO approve/deny/classify driver step is required for this branch. The old `gap:permission-approval` was the RISKY-branch primitive (answering an interactive prompt); it does not gate the safe branch.

## daemon side (daemon_capability: full)

opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go). The watcher (watcher.go) reads `part` rows and emits EventNewSession / EventActivity / EventRemoved; the parser (parser.go) maps tool `status=pending|running` → `function_call` (working), `status=completed|error` → `function_call_output`, and `step-finish reason=stop` → `turn_done` (ready). The ungated ready→working→ready arc with the no-waiting invariant is exactly what this transport already observes — PROVEN by opencode/auto-executed-tool-call (yes/full/ready), whose `write` tool ran inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state. So the daemon emits the safe-path arc this cell requires today.

## The honest caveats (why confidence 0.8, not higher)

- The config `allow` (or `--dangerously-skip-permissions`) is a STATIC policy lookup, not the in-product per-call classifier surfacing a discriminating decision. The resulting recording is byte-for-byte INDISTINGUISHABLE from auto-executed-tool-call (a generic ungated tool call). It proves the safe-path invariant (`ready→working→ready`, no waiting) but does NOT separately prove an auto-CLASSIFICATION step occurred.
- The classifier's discriminating (SELECTIVE-prompt) behavior is the RISKY half and is unobservable headlessly: a tool set to `"ask"` HANGS FOREVER in headless/server mode — the tool part stays at `status=running` and the awaiting-input Promise never resolves (GitHub anomalyco/opencode #14473, #16367). The daemon would then see perpetual `working`, never `waiting` (the ProcessOwnedStore transport has no `waiting`/permission source at all). That half is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell, which stays gap:permission-approval until a live-REPL driver can ANSWER the prompt.

## Routing

Record the safe-path arc via a settings file with an `allow` permission policy (or `--dangerously-skip-permissions`) on the existing headless driver, with the explicit caveat that the recording demonstrates the no-waiting safe-path invariant, NOT a separately-observable classification step. The maintainer should weigh whether this adds signal beyond auto-executed-tool-call; if not, the cell can stay frozen-by-choice, but the technical blocker (the driver gap) is gone for the safe branch.

## Confidence

0.8: the config permission schema (allow/ask/deny + bash wildcards) and the `--dangerously-skip-permissions` flag are read directly from opencode docs and the installed v1.14.50 `opencode run --help`; the headless `"ask"` hang is sourced to two open GitHub issues; the daemon's safe-path observability is proven by the sibling auto-executed-tool-call recording and read directly from parser.go/watcher.go. Not higher because the recording cannot separately demonstrate the CLASSIFICATION discriminator (the cell's nominal distinguishing signal) on the headless path — it can only demonstrate the no-waiting safe invariant, which auto-executed-tool-call already covers.
caveats: Driver gap REMOVED for the safe branch: opencode v1.14.50 auto-classifies via config (permission.bash/edit/… = allow/ask/deny, bash wildcards) and ships `opencode run --dangerously-skip-permissions` — the safe arc auto-executes with NO prompt, so the existing send/wait_turn headless driver records it without any approve/deny step. The prior gap:permission-approval was the RISKY-branch primitive and does not gate this cell.

The safe-path recording is indistinguishable from auto-executed-tool-call (yes/full/ready): a config `allow` is a static policy lookup, not a separately-observable per-call classifier. It proves the `ready→working→ready` no-waiting invariant, NOT that an auto-CLASSIFICATION step fired. Maintainer should decide whether it adds signal beyond auto-executed-tool-call.

The classifier's SELECTIVE (risky) half is unobservable headlessly: `"ask"` hangs forever in headless mode (tool part stuck at status=running, Promise never resolves — GitHub #14473, #16367), so the daemon would see perpetual working, never waiting. That is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell, which remains gap:permission-approval until a live-REPL driver can answer a prompt.

The ProcessOwnedStore transport has no `waiting`/permission source for opencode at all — the canonical rule-0 PermissionPending→waiting gate is a claudecode-hook path opencode's store does not feed; opencode's state arc is derived purely from part types (tool status + step-finish reason).

sources.note: permission config keys (bash/edit/read/webfetch/…) accept allow/ask/deny; bash supports per-command wildcard patterns ("git *": "allow", "git commit *": "deny") — a real config-driven per-call auto-classifier, set via the settings file (settings_file_support:true)

PROOF the risky half is unobservable headlessly: a bash permission set to "ask" hangs forever in headless/server mode — the tool call enters status=running and never completes; the awaiting-input Promise never resolves and no recoverable permission event is emitted. (Corroborated by #16367 for opencode serve+attach.)

headless path implements send + wait_turn over `opencode run`; a settings file with a permission `allow` policy (or --dangerously-skip-permissions, confirmed in `opencode run --help` v1.14.50) makes the safe call auto-execute with no prompt — NO approve/deny/classify step needed for the safe branch, so driver_capability:ready here

tool status pending/running → function_call (working), completed/error → function_call_output, step-finish reason=stop → turn_done (ready); NO permission/ask/classify/waiting branch — the daemon can emit the safe-path ready→working→ready no-waiting arc but has no waiting source to represent a gated call

ProcessOwnedStore watcher reads `part` rows → EventNewSession/EventActivity/EventRemoved; no permission/waiting concept — a headless "ask" hang would surface as a tool part stuck at status=running (perpetual working), never a waiting transition

PROOF the daemon observes the ungated safe arc on this transport (yes/full/ready): gemma's `write` tool ran inside the working span with zero waiting/approval state (ready→working→ready, no waiting). A safe-path auto-classified-permission recording would be indistinguishable from it.

sibling risky-path branch — stays gap:permission-approval (the interactive-answer primitive headless opencode lacks). This cell (the safe branch) and that one are the two halves of opencode's permission classifier; only the safe half is recordable without a live-REPL approval driver.

preconditions: opencode CLI on PATH and authenticated/configured (default model in ~/.config/opencode/opencode.json; auth is out-of-band)
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh in isolated/coexist mode, or --attach against a running daemon started with --record

the configured default model must be capable of function calling — if it emits no tool call this cell is unrecordable for a MODEL reason (opencode capabilities.json tool_calls:true), not an opencode/daemon incapability
setup: SAFE branch of opencode's config-driven permission classifier (the RISKY branch — a tool set to "ask" — is the sibling tool-gate-permission-prompt cell; it hangs forever headlessly, opencode #14473/#16367, so only the safe branch is recordable on the headless driver)

The recipe `settings` blob is seeded by drive-opencode-interactive.sh as a project-local opencode.json in the per-run cwd BEFORE the run. It pins `permission: {bash:"allow", edit:"allow"}` so the bash echo is config-CLASSIFIED-ALLOWED and auto-executes with NO prompt — this explicit allow policy is what gives the cell distinct permission-classification signal vs auto-executed-tool-call (which pins no policy and relies on the headless default). opencode loads project config before the global ~/.config/opencode config, so the policy applies to this run only and never mutates the user's global config

The driver launches a blocking `opencode run -- "<prompt>"` in a fresh per-run cwd under <staging>/cwd/ with NO --model flag (uses the config default); the send is its own subprocess that exits when the turn completes

No approve/deny/classify driver step is needed: the allow policy means the permission prompt never fires, so the existing send|wait_turn|sleep headless path records the safe arc. wait_turn is a no-op because `opencode run` already blocks until the turn ends; the ≥4s trailing sleep gives the daemon's poller one full tick to observe the working→ready settle before the run process exits

Session state lives in opencode's SQLite store (~/.local/share/opencode/opencode.db, ProcessOwnedStore), keyed on the UUID ses_… session; the bash call lands in the part table as a `tool` part (status pending→running→completed) which the parser maps to function_call / function_call_output (parser.go parseToolPart). The ProcessOwnedStore transport has NO waiting/permission source, so the no-waiting invariant holds by construction for the safe branch
The send is a crisp single-tool-forcing prompt so the small/slow local default model reliably emits exactly one auto-classified-allowed bash tool call inside the working span, then replies done

script.text: Run the shell command `echo hello` using your bash tool. Then reply with exactly: done

verify: events.jsonl contains the ses_… session born ready within ~2s of agent launch (a transient proc-<PID> presession may also appear; the turn arc and pid_discovered land on the UUID-keyed ses_… session)
events.jsonl contains a pid_discovered event bound to the ses_… session_id
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→working on the ses_… session_id when the bash-tool prompt is sent, sustained across the auto-classified-allowed bash call
the opencode SQLite store / transcript.jsonl contains a tool part (function_call / function_call_output) for the bash call — config-classified ALLOWED, so it auto-executes with no permission window
events.jsonl contains a state_transition→ready on the same ses_… session_id once the tool resolves and the turn completes (step-finish reason=stop → turn_done)
events.jsonl contains NO state_transition to waiting for the ses_… session at any point (the allow policy means the prompt never fires; the safe-path arc is ready→working→ready)

metadata.notes:
agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a
applicable: no

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: no
daemon_capability: n/a; driver_capability: n/a
applicable: no

## Scenario

`auto-classified-permission` exercises the `permission_hooks` primitive: an in-product classifier that auto-approves *safe* tool calls (state stays `ready → working → ready`, never `waiting`) but selectively gates *risky* ones into `working → waiting → working → ready` on user approval. The safe-path branch is this cell; the risky-path branch is owned by `tool-gate-permission-prompt`. The whole arc requires the agent to have a permission classifier that distinguishes safe from risky tool calls at runtime.

## Reasoning — agent layer (`no`)

pi has no permission classifier and no runtime tool-approval gate of any kind. Stock built-in tools (read, bash, edit, write) execute directly and autonomously within the turn. Three independent primary sources confirm this:

1. **`capabilities.json` declares `permission_hooks: false`.** This is the canonical capability key mapped from the scenario's *Primitive exercised* field.

2. **The pi package README explicitly lists "Permission gates and path protection" under the features pi does NOT ship** (it is extension territory), and states verbatim: **"No permission popups. Run in a container, or build your own confirmation flow with extensions inline with your environment and security requirements."** Permission gating is delegated to the user's environment (containers) or to user-authored extensions — it is not a stock, in-product behavior.

3. **Step 2.5 binary/source scan came up empty for a classifier.** pi ships as an unminified Node bundle under `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/`. Grepping the whole dist tree for `permission|auto-approv|classif|allowlist|denylist|risky|sandbox` returned only three hits, none of which is a runtime safe-vs-risky gate:
   - `cli/args.js` — the `--tools, -t <tools>` flag, a comma-separated **launch-time allowlist** of enabled tool names.
   - `modes/interactive/interactive-mode.js` — two incidental comments (a tmux query "sandbox" fallback and a clipboard-"permission" error swallow), neither related to tool gating.
   - `core/export-html/vendor/highlight.min.js` — a syntax-highlighter vendor lib (irrelevant).
   A broader scan for `grantPermission|requestPermission|needsApproval|requiresApproval|canExecute|isAllowed|confirm.*tool` found only `core/agent-session.js`'s `isAllowedTool(name) = !allowedToolNames || allowedToolNames.has(name)` — a static **set-membership check against the launch-time allowlist**, not a runtime classifier and not an interactive approval prompt. There is no safe-vs-risky branching anywhere in the bundle.

The `--tools` allowlist gates tool *availability* at process start (a tool not in the list never exists for the turn); it never pauses an in-flight turn for approval and never distinguishes "safe" from "risky" among enabled tools. That is categorically different from the runtime auto-classifier this scenario exercises. No selective `waiting` transition can ever be produced.

## Reasoning — observability layer (`n/a`)

Because the agent never produces the classified-permission event, there is nothing for irrlicht to observe. The pi adapter is `FilesUnderRoot` (`agent.go`) with a JSONL parser (`parser.go`) that maps only `session`, `model_change`, `compaction`, and `message` (roles `assistant` / `user` / `toolResult` / `bashExecution`) lines. The parser has no mapping that would emit a `waiting` state from a tool call — tool calls live inside an `assistant` (mid-turn, working) message and `toolResult` lines, all of which keep the session `working` until `stopReason=="stop"` flips it to `turn_done` (ready). Even if pi *did* classify permissions, the transcript carries no permission-classification line for the parser to key on. So `irrlicht_observes` is `n/a` — not a transport gap to be closed, but the absence of any agent-side event to observe.

## Applicability

With `agent_supports: no`, `applicable` is `no` per the return-contract table: the pipeline is frozen at the agent layer. No recipe and no recording follow. The cell unblocks only if pi adds a built-in (non-extension) permission classifier in a future release — at which point re-assess.

## Matrix drift

None. Coverage matrix already says `no / n/a` with an empty note; this assessment ratifies it and supplies the binary-scan evidence so the verdict is not re-litigated.
caveats: pi CAN restrict tools via the launch-time `--tools`/`-t` allowlist (and run inside a container), but this is a static, process-start availability gate — not a runtime safe-vs-risky classifier and not an interactive per-call approval prompt, so it produces no `waiting` transition.

Permission gating is explicitly delegated to user-authored extensions (README: "build your own confirmation flow with extensions"); a custom extension could add a classifier, but that is not stock pi and would need its own assessment.

sources.note: "permission_hooks": false — canonical capability key for this scenario's Primitive exercised
Source: FilesUnderRoot + ExactName{pi}; no permission-event transport
ParseLine maps session/model_change/compaction/message only; tool calls keep state working, no waiting mapping exists
Step 2.5 source scan: only gate is isAllowedTool(name)=!allowedToolNames||allowedToolNames.has(name) — static launch-time allowlist membership, not a runtime classifier
Step 2.5 source scan: --tools/-t is a comma-separated launch-time allowlist, not an interactive per-call permission gate
"Permission gates and path protection" listed under features pi does NOT build in; "No permission popups. Run in a container, or build your own confirmation flow with extensions."
